# jjezs_paddock ₣A9

## Character

Product design into prototype implementation. Starts with synthesis (specs capturing a rich design conversation), transitions to exploratory engineering (Tauri + Rust + cross-platform clipboard), then steady implementation of a three-tier PHI detection engine. Requires careful attention to minting discipline — this kit lives alongside RBK/JJK/BUK and must respect the prefix universe.

## Problem

Clinicians using Epic EHR need to consult Open Evidence (an AI diagnostic tool) but must not paste Protected Health Information (PHI) into it. HIPAA Safe Harbor defines 18 PHI categories that must be removed. Today, clinicians either skip the consultation or manually scrub text — both are unacceptable. No clipboard-level tool exists to automate the triage.

## Solution

Ann's PHI Clipbuddy (`apcap`) — a Tauri desktop app (macOS, later Windows) that:
1. Validates clipboard content as clinical via heuristic, clears system clipboard after consumption
2. Parses Epic "Copy All" HTML for structural context (labels, sections)
3. Runs a three-tier PHI detection engine (regex → label-anchored → dictionary)
4. Displays a triage view: RED (definite PHI), YELLOW (questionable), GREY (pass)
5. Lets clinician toggle findings — YELLOW easily, RED with deliberate friction (separate panel section)
6. Writes anonymized plain text to clipboard with category-specific placeholders (DOB→age, >89→"90+")
7. Self-updates from `/Users/Shared/apcua/` without confirmation

## Key Design Decisions

- **Anchored vs Matched**: Tier 2 (label position) and Tier 3 (dictionary identity) are independent mechanisms with distinct vocabulary — see APCPS
- **Case-insensitive**: All dictionary lookups normalize to lowercase; original case preserved in display
- **Per-word anchoring**: No multi-word merge — each word individually classified
- **Conservative bias**: False positive preferred over false negative
- **Provider/facility names**: Treated as PHI (re-identifying in combination with clinical details)
- **Clipboard always cleared** after consumption
- **Clinical content heuristic** gates processing; non-clinical clipboard shows diagnostic and clears triage

## Authoritative Documents

| Document | Owns | Path |
|----------|------|------|
| Product Spec (APCS) | UX plan, workflow, deployment, HIPAA context | `Tools/apck/APCS-Specification.md` |
| Prototype Spec (APCPS) | Detection pipeline, tech stack, dictionaries, project structure, scope boundaries | `Tools/apck/APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md` |
| Claude Context | Build/run/deploy instructions, file mappings, prefix tree, tabtargets | `Tools/apck/apck-claude-context.md` |
| Rust Coding Guide (RCG) | Naming conventions, test patterns, disciplines | `Tools/vok/vov_veiled/RCG-RustCodingGuide.md` |

**Heat ₣A9 — apck-1-proof-of-life** (racing, 6/10 done)

**Paddock**: Tauri desktop app for clinicians — intercepts Epic EHR clipboard, detects PHI via three-tier engine (regex/label-anchored/dictionary), presents triage view for review, writes anonymized text back to clipboard.

**Recent work**: Self-update watcher (₢A9AAH), three-tier detection engine (₢A9AAE), HTML parser (₢A9AAD), fixture loader (₢A9AAC).

**Next pace: ₢A9AAF — frontend-triage-view** (rough)

The docket calls for wiring the frontend to the Rust backend:
- Three UI states: initial instruction, non-clinical diagnostic, triage view
- Document preview with RED/YELLOW/GREY inline highlights
- Two-section findings panel: Questionable (YELLOW, upper) / Definite PHI (RED, lower)
- Toggle controls (ELIDE/PASS) with real-time preview updates
- Tauri IPC `consume_clipboard` command returning classified token stream
- Clipboard change detection preserving triage state on refocus

**Approach:**
1. Implement Rust-side `consume_clipboard` Tauri command that runs the detection engine and returns classified JSON
2. Update `index.html` with three-state layout and two-section findings panel
3. Update `style.css` with RED/YELLOW/GREY highlight styles
4. Update `app.js` with IPC invoke, document preview rendering, findings panel population, toggle logic, and refocus detection

**Assessment**: Sequential sonnet would be fine for the mechanical HTML/CSS/JS, but the Tauri IPC wiring and ensuring the classified token stream format bridges correctly between Rust and JS benefits from opus attention. Sequential opus — multiple files but tightly coupled data flow.

24	    let mut clipboard = arboard::Clipboard::new()
25	        .map_err(|e| format!("clipboard error: {}", e))?;
26	    clipboard.get_text().map_err(|e| format!("clipboard error: {}", e))
27	}
28	
29	fn main() {
30	    apcd::apcrl_info_now!("starting Ann's PHI Clipbuddy");
31	    apcd::apcru_update::apcru_start_watcher();
32	    tauri::Builder::default()

34	        .run(tauri::generate_context!())
35	        .unwrap_or_else(|e| apcd::apcrl_fatal_now!("tauri application error: {}", e));
36	}

1 # Ann's PHI Clipbuddy — Prototype Specification 2 3 ## Scope 4 5 This document specifies the engineering pipeline for the proof-of-life prototype. It covers detection algorithms, tech stack, data sources, project structure, and scope boundaries. It does NOT cover the user experience — see [APCS-Specification.md](APCS-Specification.md) for the UX plan. 6 7 This is a living document. The pipeline will evolve rapidly during prototype development. Changes here do not require parallel updates to the product spec. 8 9 ## Scope Boundaries 10 11 The prototype deliberately excludes: 12 13 - **No persistence** — no logs, no audit trail, no history of anonymized notes 14 - **One patient at a time** — no batch processing, no queue 15 - **No configuration** — no custom blacklists, no user preferences, no settings UI 16 - **No click-to-highlight** — clicking a finding does not scroll/highlight in the document preview (future feature) 17 - **No multi-word merge** — adjacent name hits are individually flagged, not merged into a single token 18 19 ## Tech Stack 20 21 | Layer | Crate | Version | License | Rationale | 22 |-------|-------|---------|---------|-----------| 23 | Clipboard | `arboard` | 3.x | MIT/Apache | Only crate with cross-platform HTML clipboard read (`get_html()`) | 24 | HTML parsing | `scraper` | latest | MIT | DOM + CSS selectors, built on Mozilla's html5ever | 25 | Dictionary matching | `aho-corasick` | latest | MIT/Unlicense | Single-pass multi-pattern O(n) regardless of dictionary size | 26 | Pattern matching | `regex` | latest | MIT/Apache | Structural PHI patterns | 27 | Word boundaries | `unicode-segmentation` | latest | MIT/Apache | UAX#29 compliant word boundary detection | 28 | GUI shell | `tauri` | 2.x | MIT | HTML/CSS/JS frontend, Rust backend, system webview | 29 | File watching | `notify` | latest | MIT/Apache | FSEvents on macOS, ReadDirectoryChanges on Windows | 30 31 All engine crates are pure Rust, fully Cargo-lockable on macOS and Windows. Tauri requires the system webview (WebKit on macOS, WebView2 on Windows — both ship with the OS). 32 33 ## Input: Epic "Copy All" HTML 34 35 Epic's "Copy All" places HTML on the clipboard with structural formatting: 36 37 - `Label: value` patterns for demographics and metadata 38 - Section headers for clinical categories (Chief Complaint, HPI, PMH, Medications, Vitals, Labs, Assessment/Plan) 39 - Narrative freetext in the History of Present Illness and other sections 40 - Signature blocks with provider names and timestamps 41 42 The HTML structure is semi-structured — not arbitrary prose and not fully machine-readable. This structure is the primary advantage over plain text processing: labels anchor PHI detection with high precision. 43 44 ## Clipboard Handling 45 46 ### Clinical Content Heuristic 47 48 Before processing, the engine checks whether clipboard content appears to be clinical. The check scans for known Epic/clinical label patterns: 49 50 **Positive indicators** (presence of 2+ triggers acceptance): 51 - `Patient:`, `DOB:`, `MRN:`, `Attending:`, `Facility:` 52 - `Chief Complaint:`, `History of Present Illness:`, `Assessment/Plan:` 53 - `Vitals:`, `Labs:`, `Medications:`, `Allergies:` 54 - HTML `Label:` structure characteristic of Epic formatting 55 56 If clinical content is detected, the engine proceeds to the detection pipeline and clears the system clipboard (writes empty string via `arboard::set_text("")`). 57 58 If clinical content is NOT detected, the app clears any previous triage state and returns to the initial instruction view. The app displays a brief diagnostic line showing: clipboard content length, content type (HTML/plain text), and the first ~100 characters of content. This diagnostic aids debugging when real Epic data fails the heuristic — the developer can see what was on the clipboard and update the label list accordingly. 59 60 This heuristic will be refined when tested against real Epic output. The initial label list is calibrated to pass the synthetic test fixture. 61 62 ### Clipboard Change Detection 63 64 On window focus, the engine compares the current clipboard content (byte-for-byte string comparison) against the last successfully consumed content. If identical, the existing triage state is preserved — no reprocessing. If different, the clinical content heuristic runs on the new content. Clinical notes are a few KB; storing the last consumed string for comparison has negligible memory cost at prototype scale. 65 66 ## Detection Pipeline 67 68 ### Overview 69 70 Three tiers execute in sequence. Results merge by highest severity — if a token is flagged by multiple tiers, the most severe classification wins. 71 72 ``` 73 Input: Epic HTML clipboard content 74 | 75 Parse HTML → typed spans with labels and positions 76 | 77 Tier 1: Regex scan → RED 78 | 79 Tier 2: Label-anchored extraction → RED 80 | 81 Tier 3: Dictionary blacklist/whitelist scan → YELLOW or PASS 82 | 83 Merge by highest severity 84 | 85 Output: classified token stream → frontend 86 ``` 87 88 ### Tier 1 — Regex Patterns (→ RED) 89 90 Structural patterns with low false positive rate. No database dependency. 91 92 **Known ambiguities:** The zip code regex (`\d{5}`) can match 5-digit lab values, identifiers, or dosage amounts. The date regex can match version numbers or other formatted numerics. The street address regex can match non-address text with number-word-suffix patterns. These are accepted risks for the prototype — the conservative bias (false positive > false negative) means these appear as RED items the clinician can review. Context-aware refinement is deferred. 93 94 | Pattern | Category | Regex (representative) | Notes | 95 |---------|----------|----------------------|-------| 96 | SSN | `[SSN]` | `\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}` | | 97 | Phone | `[PHONE]` | `\d{3}[-.)]\s?\d{3}[-.)]\s?\d{4}`, `\(\d{3}\)\s?\d{3}-\d{4}` | Multiple formats | 98 | Email | `[EMAIL]` | `[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[\w.-]+` | | 99 | Dates | `[DATE]` | `\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{2,4}`, `\d{1,2}-\d{1,2}-\d{2,4}`, month name patterns | Multiple formats | 100 | Street address | `[ADDRESS]` | `\d+\s+[A-Z]\w+\s+(St\|Dr\|Ave\|Blvd\|Rd\|Ln\|Way\|Ct\|Pl\|Cir\|Drive\|Street\|Avenue\|Boulevard\|Road\|Lane\|Court\|Place\|Circle)` | Followed by optional city/state/zip | 101 | Zip code | `[ADDRESS]` | `\b\d{5}(-\d{4})?\b` | Within address context | 102 | MRN (labeled) | `[MRN]` | Value following `MRN:` label | | 103 | Account (labeled) | `[ACCOUNT]` | Value following `Account` or `Acct` label | | 104 | Encounter ID (labeled) | `[ENCOUNTER_ID]` | Value following `Encounter ID:` label | | 105 106 Dates receive special handling: when a date follows a `DOB:` label, the engine computes age from the current date and emits `Age: NN` instead of `[DATE]` in the anonymized output. Per HIPAA Safe Harbor (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)(i)(C)), ages over 89 are aggregated: computed age >89 emits `Age: 90+`. 107 108 ### Tier 2 — Label-Anchored Extraction (→ RED) 109 110 Words immediately following known Epic labels are PHI regardless of dictionary membership. The label is authoritative — if Epic says `Patient: Xylophone McZephyr`, that's a name even if no database contains it. 111 112 | Label Pattern | Category | Extraction Rule | 113 |--------------|----------|-----------------| 114 | `Patient:` | `[NAME]` | All text until next label or line break | 115 | `Attending:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | 116 | `Provider:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | 117 | `Referring:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | 118 | `Facility:` | `[FACILITY]` | All text until next label or line break | 119 | `Electronically signed by:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | 120 | `Emergency contact` | `[NAME]` | Name portion, phone extracted by Tier 1 | 121 | `primary care physician` | `[PROVIDER]` | Following name (e.g., "Dr. Susan Chen") | 122 123 Label matching is case-insensitive on the label text. Value extraction extends to the next structural boundary (label, line break, or HTML element boundary). 124 125 **Per-word anchoring:** Each word in the extracted value is individually flagged as RED. "Margaret J. Thornton" after `Patient:` produces three separate **anchored** items, each classified `[NAME]`. In anonymized output: `[NAME] [NAME] [NAME]`. No multi-word merge is attempted. 126 127 Items caught by Tier 2 are **anchored** — their RED classification is final. They are not subsequently dictionary-checked. If a word is both anchored (Tier 2) and matched (Tier 3), the anchored classification takes precedence. 128 129 ### Vocabulary: Anchored vs. Matched 130 131 Two independent mechanisms flag words as potential PHI. To avoid confusion, the spec distinguishes: 132 133 - **Anchored**: Flagged by Tier 2 because of *position* — the word follows a known label. Always RED. Independent of any dictionary. 134 - **Matched**: Flagged by Tier 3 because of *identity* — the word appears in a name/location dictionary. YELLOW (unless also anchored, in which case RED wins). 135 136 A word can be both anchored and matched (e.g., "Thornton" after `Patient:` AND in the Census surname list). Anchored always takes precedence. The term "blacklist" in this spec refers specifically to the Tier 3 name/location dictionaries; Tier 2 label-anchored extraction is a separate mechanism. 137 138 ### Tier 3 — Dictionary Blacklist/Whitelist (→ YELLOW or PASS) 139 140 Catches names and locations in narrative text that lack label anchors (e.g., "her husband Robert Thornton called 911"). 141 142 **Case handling:** All dictionary lookups are case-insensitive. The aho-corasick automaton is built from lowercase dictionary entries. Input tokens are lowercased before matching. Original case is preserved in display and anonymized output. 143 144 **Process:** 145 1. Segment text into words using `unicode-segmentation` (UAX#29 boundaries) 146 2. Lowercase each token for lookup; preserve original for display 147 3. Run aho-corasick automaton against lowercased tokens (single pass, O(n)) 148 4. For each hit, check whitelist membership (also lowercase) 149 5. Classify: 150 - Blacklist hit, no whitelist hit → **YELLOW** (matched, default ELIDE) 151 - Blacklist hit AND whitelist hit → **YELLOW** (collision, default ELIDE) 152 - Whitelist hit only → **PASS** 153 - Neither list → **PASS** 154 155 **Collision resolution context signals** (applied within YELLOW to aid clinician review): 156 - Capitalization mid-sentence suggests a proper noun (name) rather than common word 157 - Adjacent blacklist hits suggest a full name ("Robert Thornton" — two hits together) 158 - Section context: names in demographics header vs. narrative HPI carry different weight 159 160 These signals inform the findings panel display but do not change the YELLOW classification in the prototype. The clinician makes the final call. 161 162 ## Dictionary Data 163 164 ### Proof-of-Life Sizing 165 166 Curated-small dictionaries that prove the architecture without requiring full datasets: 167 168 | Dictionary | Source | License | Size | File | 169 |------------|--------|---------|------|------| 170 | Surnames | US Census 2010, top 1,000 by frequency | Public domain | ~1,000 entries | `dictionaries/surnames.txt` | 171 | First names | SSA baby names, top 1,000 | Public domain | ~1,000 entries | `dictionaries/firstnames.txt` | 172 | Cities | US state capitals + top 100 cities by population | Public domain | ~150 entries | `dictionaries/cities.txt` | 173 | Medical whitelist | Hand-curated: common medications, lab tests, diagnoses, anatomy, abbreviations | Original curation | ~500 entries | `dictionaries/medical_whitelist.txt` | 174 | English whitelist | Top 5,000 common English words | Public domain | ~5,000 entries | `dictionaries/english_whitelist.txt` | 175 176 Total: ~8,000 entries, ~80KB on disk. 177 178 ### Full Dataset Sizing (Future) 179 180 | Dictionary | Source | License | Size | 181 |------------|--------|---------|------| 182 | Surnames | US Census 2010, complete | Public domain | ~160,000 | 183 | First names | SSA baby names, complete | Public domain | ~100,000 | 184 | Cities | US Census incorporated places | Public domain | ~30,000 | 185 | Medical whitelist | SNOMED-CT + RxNorm + LOINC subsets | NLM license (redistribution constraints) | ~100,000+ | 186 | English whitelist | Full dictionary | Public domain | ~170,000 | 187 188 ## Anonymization 189 190 ### Placeholder Strategy 191 192 Category-specific placeholders preserve semantic context for Open Evidence: 193 194 | PHI Category | Placeholder | Example | 195 |-------------|-------------|---------| 196 | Patient/person names | `[NAME]` | "Ms. [NAME] is a 74-year-old female" | 197 | Dates (non-DOB) | `[DATE]` | "Visit Date: [DATE]" | 198 | Date of birth | `Age: NN` or `Age: 90+` | "DOB: 03/15/1952" → "Age: 74"; ages >89 → "Age: 90+" | 199 | Medical record number | `[MRN]` | "MRN: [MRN]" | 200 | Phone numbers | `[PHONE]` | "phone: [PHONE]" | 201 | Addresses | `[ADDRESS]` | "at [ADDRESS]" | 202 | Email addresses | `[EMAIL]` | | 203 | Social Security numbers | `[SSN]` | | 204 | Facility names | `[FACILITY]` | "Seen at [FACILITY]" | 205 | Provider names | `[PROVIDER]` | "Dr. [PROVIDER]" | 206 | Account numbers | `[ACCOUNT]` | | 207 | Encounter IDs | `[ENCOUNTER_ID]` | | 208 209 ### Output Format 210 211 Plain text with preserved section structure (line breaks, section headers). HTML formatting is stripped — this is itself a privacy measure, removing embedded metadata and hidden fields. The output should be readable when pasted into Open Evidence's chat input. 212 213 ## Self-Update Mechanism 214 215 The application watches `/Users/Shared/apcua/` for new `.app` bundles using the `notify` crate (FSEvents backend on macOS). On detection: 216 217 1. Copy new bundle over current application path 218 2. Spawn `open -n ` to launch new version 219 3. `std::process::exit(0)` to terminate current process 220 221 No confirmation dialog. The watcher runs on a background thread independent of the main Tauri event loop. If the watch directory does not exist at startup, the app creates it silently. 222 223 ## Project Structure 224 225 ``` 226 Tools/apck/ 227 apck-claude-context.md # Build/run/deploy instructions (RCG reference) 228 APCS-Specification.md # Product spec (UX vision) 229 APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md # This document (pipeline engineering) 230 apcw_workbench.sh # Bash workbench 231 apcz_zipper.sh # Zipper enrollment 232 test_fixtures/ 233 epic_progress_note.html # Synthetic Epic clipboard data 234 apcd/ # Rust/Tauri source directory 235 Cargo.toml # Two [[bin]]: apcap (app), apcal (fixture loader) 236 src/ 237 apcap_main.rs # Tauri app entry point 238 apcal_main.rs # Fixture loader entry point 239 lib.rs # Engine library + test module declarations 240 apcre_engine.rs # PHI detection orchestrator 241 apcte_engine.rs # Tests for engine 242 apcrp_parse.rs # HTML clipboard parsing 243 apctp_parse.rs # Tests for parser 244 apcrm_match.rs # Dictionary/regex matching 245 apctm_match.rs # Tests for matching 246 apcrd_dictionaries.rs # Dictionary loading 247 apctd_dictionaries.rs # Tests for dictionaries 248 apcru_update.rs # Directory watcher + self-update (no unit tests) 249 ui/ 250 index.html 251 style.css 252 app.js 253 dictionaries/ 254 surnames.txt 255 firstnames.txt 256 cities.txt 257 medical_whitelist.txt 258 english_whitelist.txt 259 ``` 260 261 ## Prefix Tree 262 263 ``` 264 apc (non-terminal) 265 ├── apca (non-terminal) 266 │ ├── apcal — App Loader binary (fixture clipboard tool) 267 │ └── apcap — App Prototype binary (Tauri main) 268 ├── apcd — Rust/Tauri source directory 269 ├── apck — kit directory 270 ├── apcps — prototype specification document 271 ├── apcs — product specification document 272 ├── apcu (non-terminal) 273 │ └── apcua — update staging directory (/Users/Shared/apcua/) 274 ├── apcw — workbench 275 └── apcz — zipper 276 ``` 277 278 Rust source file prefixes follow RCG: `{cipher}r{classifier}_{name}.rs` where cipher is `apc`. Test files: `{cipher}t{classifier}_{name}.rs` — classifier matches between source and test. See RCG (Rust Coding Guide) for full naming conventions, including `z`-prefix internal declarations, string boundary discipline, and constant discipline. 279 280 `lib.rs` wiring: 281 ```rust 282 pub mod apcre_engine; 283 pub mod apcrp_parse; 284 pub mod apcrm_match; 285 pub mod apcrd_dictionaries; 286 pub mod apcru_update; 287 288 #[cfg(test)] mod apcte_engine; 289 #[cfg(test)] mod apctp_parse; 290 #[cfg(test)] mod apctm_match; 291 #[cfg(test)] mod apctd_dictionaries; 292 ``` 293 294 ## Tabtargets 295 296 | Tabtarget | Colophon | Purpose | 297 |-----------|----------|---------| 298 | `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh` | `apcw-b` | `cargo tauri build` (release) | 299 | `tt/apcw-r.Run.sh` | `apcw-r` | `cargo tauri dev` (local development) | 300 | `tt/apcw-d.Deploy.sh` | `apcw-d` | Build + scp to `anns-macbook-air:/Users/Shared/apcua/` | 301 | `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.sh` | `apcw-fl` | Run `apcal` to load fixture HTML onto clipboard | 302 | `tt/apcw-t.Test.sh` | `apcw-t` | `cargo test` in `apcd/` | 303

ages >89 → "Age: 90+" | | Medical record number | `[MRN]` | "MRN: [MRN]" | | Phone numbers | `[PHONE]` | "phone: [PHONE]" | | Addresses | `[ADDRESS]` | "at [ADDRESS]" | | Email addresses | `[EMAIL]` | | | Social Security numbers | `[SSN]` | | | Facility names | `[FACILITY]` | "Seen at [FACILITY]" | | Provider names | `[PROVIDER]` | "Dr. [PROVIDER]" | | Account numbers | `[ACCOUNT]` | | | Encounter IDs | `[ENCOUNTER_ID]` | | ### Output Format Plain text with preserved section structure (line breaks, section headers). HTML formatting is stripped — this is itself a privacy measure, removing embedded metadata and hidden fields. The output should be readable when pasted into Open Evidence's chat input. ## Self-Update Mechanism The application watches `/Users/Shared/apcua/` for new `.app` bundles using the `notify` crate (FSEvents backend on macOS). On detection: 1. Copy new bundle over current application path 2. Spawn `open -n ` to launch new version 3. `std::process::exit(0)` to terminate current process No confirmation dialog. The watcher runs on a background thread independent of the main Tauri event loop. If the watch directory does not exist at startup, the app creates it silently. ## Project Structure ``` Tools/apck/ apck-claude-context.md # Build/run/deploy instructions (RCG reference) APCS-Specification.md # Product spec (UX vision) APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md # This document (pipeline engineering) apcw_workbench.sh # Bash workbench apcz_zipper.sh # Zipper enrollment test_fixtures/ epic_progress_note.html # Synthetic Epic clipboard data apcd/ # Rust/Tauri source directory Cargo.toml # Two [[bin]]: apcap (app), apcal (fixture loader) src/ apcap_main.rs # Tauri app entry point apcal_main.rs # Fixture loader entry point lib.rs # Engine library + test module declarations apcre_engine.rs # PHI detection orchestrator apcte_engine.rs # Tests for engine apcrp_parse.rs # HTML clipboard parsing apctp_parse.rs # Tests for parser apcrm_match.rs # Dictionary/regex matching apctm_match.rs # Tests for matching apcrd_dictionaries.rs # Dictionary loading apctd_dictionaries.rs # Tests for dictionaries apcru_update.rs # Directory watcher + self-update (no unit tests) ui/ index.html style.css app.js dictionaries/ surnames.txt firstnames.txt cities.txt medical_whitelist.txt english_whitelist.txt ``` ## Prefix Tree ``` apc (non-terminal) ├── apca (non-terminal) │ ├── apcal — App Loader binary (fixture clipboard tool) │ └── apcap — App Prototype binary (Tauri main) ├── apcd — Rust/Tauri source directory ├── apck — kit directory ├── apcps — prototype specification document ├── apcs — product specification document ├── apcu (non-terminal) │ └── apcua — update staging directory (/Users/Shared/apcua/) ├── apcw — workbench └── apcz — zipper ``` Rust source file prefixes follow RCG: `{cipher}r{classifier}_{name}.rs` where cipher is `apc`. Test files: `{cipher}t{classifier}_{name}.rs` — classifier matches between source and test. See RCG (Rust Coding Guide) for full naming conventions, including `z`-prefix internal declarations, string boundary discipline, and constant discipline. `lib.rs` wiring: ```rust pub mod apcre_engine; pub mod apcrp_parse; pub mod apcrm_match; pub mod apcrd_dictionaries; pub mod apcru_update; #[cfg(test)] mod apcte_engine; #[cfg(test)] mod apctp_parse; #[cfg(test)] mod apctm_match; #[cfg(test)] mod apctd_dictionaries; ``` ## Tabtargets | Tabtarget | Colophon | Purpose | |-----------|----------|---------| | `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh` | `apcw-b` | `cargo tauri build` (release) | | `tt/apcw-r.Run.sh` | `apcw-r` | `cargo tauri dev` (local development) | | `tt/apcw-d.Deploy.sh` | `apcw-d` | Build + scp to `anns-macbook-air:/Users/Shared/apcua/` | | `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.sh` | `apcw-fl` | Run `apcal` to load fixture HTML onto clipboard | | `tt/apcw-t.Test.sh` | `apcw-t` | `cargo test` in `apcd/` |

19	//! Fixture loader — writes synthetic Epic clipboard HTML for testing.
20	//! Usage: cargo run --bin apcal [fixture-file]
21	
22	// RCG output discipline: all emission via apcrl_*! — no direct println!/eprintln!
23	
24	fn main() {
25	    let args: Vec<String> = std::env::args().collect();
26	    if args.len() != 2 {
27	        apcd::apcrl_fatal_now!("usage: apcal <fixture-file-path>");
28	    }
29	    let path = &args[1];
30	
31	    let html = std::fs::read_to_string(path)
32	        .unwrap_or_else(|e| apcd::apcrl_fatal_now!("failed to read {}: {}", path, e));
33	
34	    let mut clipboard = arboard::Clipboard::new()
35	        .unwrap_or_else(|e| apcd::apcrl_fatal_now!("failed to open clipboard: {}", e));
36	
37	    clipboard.set_html(&html, Some(&html))
38	        .unwrap_or_else(|e| apcd::apcrl_fatal_now!("failed to set clipboard HTML: {}", e));
39	
40	    apcd::apcrl_info_now!("loaded {} onto clipboard as HTML ({} bytes)", path, html.len());
41	}

content.text:
Everything compiles and all 65 tests pass. Here's what was implemented:

**Rust backend:**
- `Cargo.toml` — added `serde` dependency with derive feature
- `apcrm_match.rs` — added `Serialize` derives with appropriate `rename_all` to `apcrm_Severity` (lowercase), `apcrm_Mechanism` (lowercase), `apcrm_PhiCategory` (snake_case), `apcrm_Finding`
- `apcap_main.rs` — new `consume_clipboard` Tauri command:
  - `OnceLock<apcrd_Dictionaries>` for one-time dictionary loading
  - `Mutex<Option<String>>` for clipboard change detection
  - Returns tagged JSON: `unchanged` (preserve UI), `clinical` (findings + plain_text), `non_clinical` (diagnostic)
  - Clears system clipboard after clinical consumption, tracks cleared state so refocus → unchanged

**Frontend:**
- `index.html` — two-state layout: instruction (with diagnostic line) and triage (preview + findings panel)
- `style.css` — RED (`#ff000030` bg, `red` border), YELLOW (`#ffff0030` bg, `orange` border), GREY (`#999` text) highlights; scrollable findings sections; toggle buttons styled by state
- `app.js` — focus handler invokes `consume_clipboard`, three-way dispatch (`unchanged`/`clinical`/`non_clinical`), document preview renders plain_text with inline `<span>` highlights at finding offsets, findings panel splits YELLOW (upper, Questionable) from RED (lower, Definite PHI), each entry has text/category/toggle, toggle flips elide↔pass and re-renders preview in real time

**Limitation noted:** arboard 3.x has no `get_html()`, so clipboard reads use `get_text()`. The fixture loader already sets alt_text=html, so this works for prototype testing. Real Epic integration will need platform-specific HTML clipboard reading.

Ready to wrap ₢A9AAF?
